name: Xiaolongbao
desc: Shanghai-style soup dumplings with pork filling and rich broth inside
steps: Make aspic by chilling pork stock with gelatin, chop into cubes.
Mix pork with ginger, soy sauce, sesame oil, scallions, and aspic cubes.
Knead dough, rest 30 min, roll thin wrappers and pleat around filling.
Steam dumplings for 8-10 minutes until skin is translucent.
tips: Serve on cabbage leaves to prevent sticking.
Dip in black vinegar with ginger shreds.

name: Sichuan Boiled Fish (Shui Zhu Yu)
desc: Tender white fish poached in fiery chili oil broth with bean sprouts
steps: Blanch bean sprouts and place in a deep bowl.
Poach fish slices in seasoned broth until just cooked, lay over sprouts.
Fry dried chilies and Sichuan peppercorns in oil until fragrant.
Pour sizzling oil over the fish and serve immediately.
tips: Use firm white fish like bass or tilapia so it holds together.
Adjust chili quantity to your heat tolerance.

name: Cantonese Roast Duck
desc: Crispy-skinned roast duck glazed with maltose and five-spice
steps: Rub duck cavity with five-spice, ginger, and star anise.
Blanch duck in boiling water, then coat skin with maltose-vinegar glaze.
Hang dry for 4 hours or use a fan to dry the skin.
Roast at 200C for 90 minutes, basting occasionally until skin is crisp.
tips: Air-drying the skin is the key to crispiness.
Serve with steamed buns and hoisin sauce.

name: Har Gow (Crystal Shrimp Dumplings)
desc: Translucent steamed shrimp dumplings, a classic dim sum staple
steps: Chop shrimp coarsely, mix with diced bamboo shoots, sesame oil, and pepper.
Make dough with wheat starch and boiling water, knead until smooth.
Roll thin wrappers, fill with shrimp mixture, pleat edges shut.
Steam for 6-8 minutes until wrappers turn translucent.
tips: Use boiling water for the dough to activate the starch.
Work quickly as the dough dries out fast.

name: Char Siu Bao (BBQ Pork Buns)
desc: Fluffy steamed buns filled with sweet Cantonese BBQ pork
steps: Make dough with flour, yeast, sugar, and warm water; let rise 1 hour.
Dice char siu pork, cook with oyster sauce, hoisin, sugar, and cornstarch slurry.
Divide dough into portions, flatten, fill with pork, and pinch shut.
Steam buns for 12 minutes until puffy and cooked through.
tips: Let buns rest 5 minutes before steaming so they puff evenly.
Use cake flour for a softer, whiter bun.

name: Sichuan Dry Pot Chicken (Gan Guo Ji)
desc: Spicy dry-fried chicken with vegetables and numbing Sichuan peppers
steps: Cut chicken into bite-size pieces, marinate with soy sauce 15 minutes.
Fry potato slices until golden, set aside.
Stir-fry chicken with ginger, chilies, and peppercorns until crispy.
Add celery and potato back, toss together and serve in a hot pot.
tips: Keep the heat high for a dry, slightly charred texture.
Add lotus root or cauliflower for variety.

name: Zhajiangmian (Beijing Fried Sauce Noodles)
desc: Thick wheat noodles topped with savory fermented bean paste and pork sauce
steps: Cook noodles according to package, drain and rinse with cold water.
Fry ground pork until browned, add bean pastes with a splash of water.
Simmer sauce until thick and glossy, about 5 minutes.
Top noodles with sauce, julienned cucumber, and scallions.
tips: Tian mian jiang (sweet bean paste) is the authentic sauce base.
Shredded radish and edamame are traditional garnishes.

name: Kung Pao Shrimp
desc: Spicy stir-fried shrimp with peanuts, dried chilies, and Sichuan pepper
steps: Marinate shrimp with cornstarch and a splash of soy sauce.
Stir-fry dried chilies and peppercorns in oil until fragrant.
Add shrimp, cook until pink, then add sauce of soy, vinegar, and sugar.
Toss in peanuts and scallions, serve immediately.
tips: Devein shrimp but keep tails on for presentation.
Toast peanuts first for extra crunch.

name: Hunan Orange Chicken
desc: Crispy fried chicken in tangy orange and chili glaze, Hunan-style
steps: Cut chicken into pieces, coat in cornstarch.
Deep fry chicken until golden and crispy, drain on paper towels.
Stir-fry garlic and chilies, add orange juice, zest, and soy sauce.
Toss crispy chicken in the glaze and serve hot.
tips: Use fresh orange juice for the best flavor.
Double-fry the chicken for extra crispiness.

name: Mapo Eggplant
desc: Silky eggplant braised in spicy doubanjiang and Sichuan pepper sauce
steps: Cut eggplant into chunks, salt and drain for 10 minutes.
Fry eggplant until softened and lightly browned.
Stir-fry garlic, ginger, and doubanjiang until fragrant.
Add eggplant back with soy sauce and a splash of water, braise 5 minutes.
tips: Salting draws out moisture for better texture.
Garnish with Sichuan pepper oil for extra numbing heat.

name: Tomato and Egg Stir-Fry
desc: Silky scrambled eggs with juicy tomatoes in sweet-savory sauce
steps: Beat eggs with a pinch of salt, scramble in hot oil until just set, remove.
Cook diced tomatoes in the same pan until saucy and soft.
Return eggs to the pan, add sugar, toss gently to combine.
Garnish with scallions and serve with rice.
tips: Use ripe, juicy tomatoes for the best sauce.
Don't overcook the eggs; they should be soft and custardy.

name: Sichuan Wontons in Chili Oil (Hong You Chao Shou)
desc: Pork wontons drenched in spicy, numbing chili oil sauce
steps: Mix pork with soy sauce, sesame oil, and minced ginger for filling.
Wrap small amounts of filling in wonton skins, sealing edges with water.
Boil wontons until they float, about 3-4 minutes.
Toss in sauce of chili oil, soy sauce, garlic, sesame paste, and peppercorn.
tips: Make extra and freeze uncooked wontons for quick meals.
Adjust chili oil to your spice preference.

name: Salt and Pepper Tofu
desc: Crispy fried tofu cubes tossed with chili, garlic, and Sichuan salt
steps: Press tofu, cut into cubes, coat generously in cornstarch.
Deep fry until golden and crispy on all sides.
Stir-fry garlic, chilies, and scallions briefly.
Toss crispy tofu with the aromatics and peppercorn salt.
tips: Press tofu well for maximum crispiness.
Sichuan peppercorn salt is made by toasting peppercorns with salt and grinding.

name: Cantonese Steamed Spare Ribs with Black Beans
desc: Tender pork ribs steamed with fermented black beans and garlic
steps: Chop ribs into bite-size pieces, rinse and pat dry.
Marinate with mashed black beans, garlic, soy sauce, sugar, and cornstarch.
Arrange on a plate, top with sliced chili.
Steam over high heat for 20 minutes until tender.
tips: Rinse fermented black beans briefly to reduce saltiness.
This is a classic dim sum dish.

name: Bang Bang Chicken (Bon Bon Ji)
desc: Cold shredded chicken in creamy sesame-chili sauce, Sichuan appetizer
steps: Poach chicken breast in gently simmering water for 15 minutes, cool.
Shred chicken by hand into thin strips.
Mix sesame paste, chili oil, soy sauce, vinegar, and sugar into a smooth sauce.
Arrange chicken over sliced cucumber, drizzle with sauce.
tips: Poach gently to keep the chicken tender and juicy.
Let chicken cool in the poaching liquid for extra moisture.

name: Drunken Chicken (Zui Ji)
desc: Cold poached chicken marinated in Shaoxing wine, a Shanghai classic
steps: Poach chicken legs with ginger and scallions until cooked through.
Plunge into ice water to stop cooking and tighten the skin.
Submerge chicken in mixture of Shaoxing wine, poaching broth, salt, and sugar.
Refrigerate overnight, chop and serve cold.
tips: Must marinate at least 8 hours for the wine flavor to penetrate.
Use the best quality Shaoxing wine you can find.

name: Braised Sea Cucumber with Scallions (Cong Shao Hai Shen)
desc: Shandong banquet dish of sea cucumber braised with caramelized scallions
steps: Blanch sea cucumber in ginger water to remove any odor.
Fry scallion whites in oil until deeply caramelized and fragrant.
Add sea cucumber, soy sauce, wine, sugar, and stock; braise 20 minutes.
Reduce sauce until glossy, garnish with scallion greens.
tips: Sea cucumber must be fully rehydrated over several days before cooking.
This is considered a prestige dish in Shandong cuisine.

name: La Zi Ji (Chongqing Chicken with Chilies)
desc: Crispy fried chicken pieces buried in a mountain of dried chilies
steps: Cut chicken into small cubes, marinate with soy sauce and cornstarch.
Deep fry chicken until golden and very crispy.
In a wok, fry dried chilies and peppercorns until darkened and fragrant.
Add chicken back, toss vigorously so flavors coat every piece.
tips: The chilies are for flavor, not eating — pick around them.
Sichuan peppercorns give the characteristic numbing sensation.

name: Three Cup Chicken (San Bei Ji)
desc: Taiwanese-Chinese braised chicken with soy sauce, sesame oil, and rice wine
steps: Cut chicken into bone-in pieces, pat dry.
Fry chicken in sesame oil until golden on all sides.
Add garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and rice wine; braise covered for 20 minutes.
Uncover, reduce sauce until thick, toss in basil leaves and serve.
tips: Equal parts soy, sesame oil, and wine is the classic 'three cups' ratio.
Thai basil is essential — do not substitute with Italian basil.

name: Squirrel-Shaped Mandarin Fish (Song Shu Gui Yu)
desc: Jiangsu classic: scored fish deep-fried into a squirrel shape with sweet-sour sauce
steps: Score fish flesh in a crosshatch pattern without cutting the skin.
Coat fish heavily in cornstarch, shaking off excess.
Deep fry at high heat until flesh puffs out like a squirrel tail.
Pour hot sweet-sour sauce with peas and shrimp over the fish.
tips: The crosshatch scoring must be deep and precise for the puff effect.
Pour the sauce tableside while sizzling for dramatic presentation.

name: Ants Climbing a Tree (Ma Yi Shang Shu)
desc: Glass noodles stir-fried with seasoned pork mince in spicy bean sauce
steps: Soak glass noodles in warm water until pliable, drain.
Stir-fry pork mince until browned, add doubanjiang and ginger.
Add noodles and soy sauce, toss until noodles absorb all the sauce.
Garnish with scallions and serve.
tips: The 'ants' are the pork bits clinging to the noodle 'trees'.
Don't oversoak the noodles or they'll become mushy.

name: Yu Xiang Shredded Pork (Fish-Fragrant Pork)
desc: Sichuan sweet-sour-spicy shredded pork with wood ear mushrooms and bamboo
steps: Shred pork, marinate with soy sauce and cornstarch.
Soak wood ear mushrooms, shred along with bamboo shoots.
Stir-fry pork until just cooked, add doubanjiang and garlic.
Add vegetables, pour in sweet-sour sauce, toss until glossy.
tips: Despite the name, there is no fish — the flavor profile mimics fish seasoning.
Balance of sweet, sour, salty, and spicy is key.

name: Beggar's Chicken (Jiao Hua Ji)
desc: Whole chicken stuffed and wrapped in lotus leaf and clay, then baked
steps: Season chicken inside and out with wine, soy sauce, and five-spice.
Stuff cavity with ginger and scallions.
Wrap tightly in lotus leaves, then encase in a thick layer of clay or salt dough.
Bake at 180C for 2 hours, crack open the clay at the table.
tips: If no clay, use a thick salt dough crust as a substitute.
The lotus leaf imparts a unique smoky-sweet fragrance.

name: Dong Po Pork (Dongpo Rou)
desc: Hangzhou braised pork belly slow-cooked until melt-in-mouth tender
steps: Cut pork belly into large squares, blanch in boiling water.
Line pot with scallions and ginger, place pork skin-side down.
Add soy sauce, wine, and sugar; bring to a boil, then simmer on low for 2 hours.
Flip pork skin-side up, serve with the reduced braising liquid.
tips: Low and slow is essential — do not rush the braise.
Named after the Song Dynasty poet Su Dongpo.

name: Tea-Smoked Duck (Zhang Cha Ya)
desc: Sichuan duck smoked with tea leaves, camphor, and rice for deep aroma
steps: Rub duck with salt, five-spice, and peppercorns; cure overnight in fridge.
Steam duck for 40 minutes until cooked through.
Line a wok with foil, add tea, rice, and sugar; smoke duck over medium heat 15 minutes.
Optionally deep-fry briefly for crispy skin before serving.
tips: Smoke in a well-ventilated area or use a range hood.
The three smoking ingredients give the characteristic complex flavor.

name: Clay Pot Rice (Bo Zai Fan)
desc: Cantonese rice cooked in a clay pot with Chinese sausage and crispy rice crust
steps: Soak rice 30 minutes, place in oiled clay pot with water and ginger.
Cook on medium heat until water is absorbed, lay sausage slices on top.
Cover and cook on low 10 minutes to form the crispy rice crust (guo ba).
Drizzle soy sauce and sesame oil, add blanched bok choy and fried egg.
tips: The prized part is the crispy scorched rice on the bottom.
Can substitute with a heavy-bottomed pot if no clay pot available.

name: Hainanese Chicken Rice
desc: Poached chicken served with fragrant rice cooked in chicken fat and broth
steps: Poach whole chicken in ginger-scallion water until just cooked, ice bath immediately.
Cook rice in chicken fat with garlic, using the poaching broth as liquid.
Chop chicken into pieces, arrange over cucumber slices.
Serve with chili sauce, ginger paste, and dark soy on the side.
tips: Ice bath after poaching gives the chicken silky, gelatinous skin.
The rice should be fragrant and slightly oily from the chicken fat.

name: Cold Sesame Noodles (Liang Mian)
desc: Chilled wheat noodles tossed in creamy sesame paste and chili oil dressing
steps: Cook noodles, rinse under cold water and drain well.
Whisk sesame paste with soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, and chili oil until smooth.
Toss noodles with the sauce until evenly coated.
Top with shredded cucumber and serve cold.
tips: Thin the sauce with a splash of noodle cooking water if too thick.
Add crushed peanuts for extra texture.

name: Beef Chow Fun (Gan Chao Niu He)
desc: Cantonese stir-fried flat rice noodles with beef and bean sprouts
steps: Slice beef thinly, marinate with soy sauce and cornstarch.
Stir-fry beef on highest heat until just seared, set aside.
Add noodles to the wok with soy sauces, toss vigorously for wok hei.
Return beef, add bean sprouts and scallions, toss 30 seconds and serve.
tips: Maximum heat (wok hei) is essential for this dish.
Separate stuck noodles gently before cooking.

name: Smashed Cucumber Salad (Pai Huang Gua)
desc: Crunchy smashed cucumbers in garlic, chili oil, and black vinegar dressing
steps: Smash cucumbers with the flat side of a knife, break into rough pieces.
Salt cucumbers and let drain for 10 minutes, squeeze out water.
Mix garlic, chili oil, vinegar, sesame oil, and sugar into a dressing.
Toss cucumbers with the dressing and serve immediately.
tips: Smashing creates irregular surfaces that grab more sauce.
Serve within 30 minutes or cucumbers become watery.

name: Singapore Noodles (Xing Zhou Mi Fen)
desc: Curry-spiced rice vermicelli stir-fried with shrimp, pork, and peppers
steps: Soak rice vermicelli in warm water until pliable, drain.
Scramble eggs, set aside. Stir-fry shrimp and sliced pork until cooked.
Add vermicelli and curry powder, toss on high heat.
Mix in bell pepper, bean sprouts, and egg, serve immediately.
tips: Despite the name, this dish was invented in Hong Kong.
Use Madras curry powder for the most authentic flavor.

name: Chinese Steamed Egg Custard (Zheng Dan)
desc: Silky smooth steamed egg custard with soy sauce and sesame oil
steps: Beat eggs gently, mix with warm water (1:1.5 ratio), strain through a sieve.
Pour into a heat-proof bowl, cover with plastic wrap.
Steam on medium-low heat for 12-15 minutes until just set.
Drizzle with soy sauce, sesame oil, and scallions.
tips: Straining the mixture removes air bubbles for a silky texture.
Steam on low heat — high heat causes bubbles and a rough surface.

name: Xinjiang Cumin Lamb Ribs
desc: Grilled lamb ribs seasoned with cumin, chili flakes, and Sichuan pepper
steps: Cut ribs into individual pieces, pat dry.
Toast cumin, chili, and peppercorns, grind coarsely.
Rub ribs with oil, garlic, salt, and spice mix.
Grill or pan-fry over high heat until charred and cooked through.
tips: These are a popular Xinjiang street food sold at night markets.
Lamb shoulder chops work as a substitute for ribs.

name: Wood Ear Mushroom Salad (Liang Ban Mu Er)
desc: Cold wood ear mushrooms dressed with vinegar, chili, and cilantro
steps: Soak wood ear mushrooms in warm water 30 minutes, trim tough stems.
Blanch mushrooms in boiling water 1 minute, drain and cool.
Toss with garlic, chili oil, vinegar, soy sauce, and cilantro.
Serve cold as an appetizer.
tips: Wood ears should be crunchy — do not overcook.
A classic Northern Chinese cold dish served before the main meal.

name: Suan La Fen (Hot and Sour Sweet Potato Noodles)
desc: Chewy sweet potato noodles in tangy, spicy broth with peanuts and pickles
steps: Soak sweet potato noodles in hot water until chewy-soft.
Mix chili oil, vinegar, soy sauce in a bowl to make the broth base.
Add noodles to the bowl, ladle hot water or stock over them.
Top with crushed peanuts, preserved greens, and scallions.
tips: Chongqing-style calls for extra chili oil and Sichuan pepper.
Adjust vinegar-to-chili ratio to your preference.

name: Gua Bao (Braised Pork Belly Buns)
desc: Steamed folded buns stuffed with braised pork belly, pickled greens, and peanuts
steps: Braise pork belly with soy sauce, five-spice, sugar, and water for 45 minutes until tender.
Steam bao buns until fluffy and warm.
Slice pork belly, tuck into buns with pickled greens, peanuts, and cilantro.
Serve immediately while buns are warm.
tips: Gua bao is popular street food across Taiwan and China.
The contrast of rich pork with tangy pickles is essential.

name: Fish-Fragrant Eggplant (Yu Xiang Qie Zi)
desc: Sichuan braised eggplant in sweet, sour, and spicy sauce with no fish
steps: Cut eggplant into strips, fry in oil until softened and golden.
Stir-fry doubanjiang, garlic, and ginger until fragrant.
Add eggplant back with vinegar, sugar, soy sauce, and a splash of water.
Simmer until sauce is thick, garnish with scallions.
tips: Yu xiang (fish-fragrant) is a Sichuan flavor profile, not a fish dish.
The balance of sweet, sour, salty, and spicy should be equal.

name: Char Kway Teow
desc: Smoky stir-fried flat rice noodles with shrimp, Chinese sausage, and egg
steps: Heat wok until smoking, add oil and fry sausage slices.
Push aside, crack eggs and scramble roughly.
Add noodles and soy sauces, toss on highest heat for wok hei.
Add shrimp, bean sprouts, and chives, toss 1 minute and serve.
tips: Wok hei (breath of the wok) is the signature smoky flavor.
Use the widest, hottest burner you have.

name: Chicken Lettuce Wraps (Sang Choy Bao)
desc: Cantonese minced chicken in crispy lettuce cups with water chestnuts
steps: Stir-fry ground chicken with garlic until cooked through.
Add diced water chestnuts, hoisin sauce, soy sauce, and sesame oil.
Cook until sauce is absorbed and mixture is slightly dry.
Spoon into crisp lettuce cups and serve immediately.
tips: Water chestnuts add essential crunch to the filling.
Use butter lettuce if iceberg is unavailable.

name: Turnip Cake (Lo Bak Go)
desc: Pan-fried Cantonese radish cake with Chinese sausage and dried shrimp
steps: Grate daikon, cook in a pot until soft, reserving the liquid.
Mix rice flour with daikon liquid into a batter, stir in diced sausage and shrimp.
Pour into a greased pan, steam for 45 minutes until firm.
Cool, slice, and pan-fry until golden and crispy on both sides.
tips: A must-have at dim sum and during Chinese New Year.
Better the next day after refrigerating — firms up for crispier frying.

name: Dan Dan Chicken Noodles
desc: Spicy Sichuan noodles with ground chicken in sesame-chili sauce
steps: Cook noodles, drain and divide into bowls.
Brown ground chicken with preserved vegetables until crispy.
Mix sesame paste, chili oil, peppercorn, and soy sauce in each bowl.
Top noodles with chicken crumbles, toss and eat.
tips: Ya cai (preserved mustard stems) is the traditional vegetable used.
Adjust the ratio of sesame to chili for your preference.

name: Century Egg Tofu
desc: Cold silken tofu topped with century egg, soy sauce, and sesame oil
steps: Gently unmold silken tofu onto a plate.
Dice century eggs and arrange on top.
Drizzle with soy sauce, sesame oil, and chili oil.
Garnish with scallions and serve cold.
tips: A classic Chinese appetizer that requires zero cooking.
Use Japanese silken tofu for the smoothest texture.

name: Twice-Cooked Tofu (Hui Guo Doufu)
desc: Vegetarian take on the Sichuan classic with pan-fried tofu and leeks
steps: Slice tofu thickly, pan-fry until golden on both sides.
Stir-fry leek slices and garlic until softened.
Add doubanjiang and sweet bean paste, fry until oil turns red.
Add tofu back, toss gently with soy sauce until well coated.
tips: The original uses pork belly — tofu is the vegetarian adaptation.
Doubanjiang from Pixian county is considered the best.

name: Pineapple Bun (Bo Lo Bao)
desc: Hong Kong sweet bun with a crumbly cookie crust, no actual pineapple
steps: Make bread dough with flour, yeast, sugar, egg, and milk; let rise 1 hour.
Make cookie topping by creaming butter, sugar, egg, flour, and custard powder.
Shape dough into buns, press a disc of cookie topping on each.
Score topping in crosshatch, bake at 180C for 15 minutes until golden.
tips: Named for the crackled top that resembles pineapple skin.
For the classic Hong Kong style, slice open and add a cold butter slab.

name: Hong Kong Egg Tart (Dan Tat)
desc: Flaky pastry shells filled with silky smooth egg custard
steps: Make pastry by rubbing butter into flour, bind with cold water, chill 30 minutes.
Whisk eggs, sugar, warm milk, and vanilla, strain for smoothness.
Press pastry into tart molds, fill with custard to 80% full.
Bake at 200C for 18-20 minutes until custard is just set with slight wobble.
tips: Strain the custard twice for the smoothest texture.
A dim sum classic — must be served warm.

name: Red Bean Soup (Hong Dou Tang)
desc: Cantonese sweet red bean soup with dried tangerine peel, served warm or cold
steps: Soak red beans overnight, drain and rinse.
Simmer beans with tangerine peel in water for 45 minutes until soft.
Add rock sugar, stir until dissolved.
Serve warm with a drizzle of coconut milk.
tips: A traditional Cantonese dessert soup (tong sui).
Can be served chilled in summer.

name: Jianbing (Chinese Breakfast Crepe)
desc: Savory street food crepe with egg, crispy wonton, and hoisin-chili sauce
steps: Mix flours with water into a thin batter, spread on a hot flat griddle.
Crack an egg on top, spread it thin, sprinkle scallions.
Flip, spread hoisin and chili sauce, place crispy wonton in the center.
Fold crepe over the filling and serve immediately.
tips: The most popular street breakfast in Northern China.
Mung bean flour gives the characteristic flavor.

name: Soy Milk with Youtiao
desc: Fresh warm soy milk paired with golden fried dough sticks
steps: Soak soybeans overnight, blend with water and strain for fresh soy milk.
Simmer soy milk, sweeten lightly with sugar.
Make dough with flour, baking powder, salt; rest 2 hours.
Stretch dough into strips, deep-fry in pairs until golden and puffy.
tips: A classic Chinese breakfast combination.
Dip youtiao into soy milk — sweet or savory soy milk both work.

name: Sheng Jian Bao (Shanghai Pan-Fried Buns)
desc: Pan-fried pork buns with crispy bottoms and juicy soup inside
steps: Make yeast dough, let rise 1 hour until doubled.
Mix pork with ginger, soy sauce, sesame oil, and chilled stock for filling.
Fill and pleat buns, rest 15 minutes.
Pan-fry in oiled skillet with lid and water until bottoms are golden crisp.
tips: The combination of frying and steaming gives the signature crispy-fluffy texture.
Sprinkle sesame seeds and scallions on top before serving.

name: Malatang (Sichuan Spicy Hot Pot Soup)
desc: Build-your-own spicy soup with skewered vegetables, tofu, and meats
steps: Dissolve ma la paste in boiling water or stock to make spicy broth.
Prepare ingredients: slice tofu, clean vegetables, soak noodles.
Cook ingredients in the broth by type — hard vegetables first, leafy greens last.
Ladle into bowls, drizzle with sesame paste.
tips: Malatang is the street food version of Sichuan hot pot.
Customize spice level by adjusting the amount of ma la paste.

name: Stir-Fried Water Spinach with Garlic (Chao Kong Xin Cai)
desc: Wok-fried water spinach with garlic, a staple Cantonese vegetable dish
steps: Cut water spinach into 3-inch pieces, separating stems from leaves.
Heat wok until smoking, add oil and garlic, fry 10 seconds.
Add stems first, stir-fry 1 minute, then add leaves.
Season with soy sauce, salt, and chili, toss 30 seconds and serve.
tips: Cook on the highest heat possible for the best wok hei.
Hollow stems cook slower than leaves — stagger the timing.

name: Kung Pao Tofu
desc: Sichuan stir-fried tofu with peanuts, chilies, and sweet-savory sauce
steps: Press and cube tofu, coat lightly in cornstarch and pan-fry until golden.
Stir-fry chilies and peppercorns in oil until fragrant.
Add sauce of soy, vinegar, and sugar, then the tofu.
Toss in peanuts and scallions, serve immediately.
tips: Press tofu thoroughly for the best crispy texture.
A satisfying vegetarian version of the Sichuan classic.

name: Sichuan Hot Pot
desc: Communal spicy broth pot for cooking meats, seafood, and vegetables at the table
steps: Dissolve hot pot base in boiling water in a communal pot.
Prepare platters of thinly sliced meats, tofu, vegetables, and noodles.
Bring broth to a simmer at the table, cook ingredients by dipping.
Make dipping sauce with sesame oil, garlic, cilantro, and chili.
tips: Thinly sliced meats cook in seconds — don't overcook.
Split pot (yuan yang) lets you have spicy and mild sides.

name: White Cut Chicken (Bai Qie Ji)
desc: Cantonese poached chicken with silky skin, served with ginger-scallion oil
steps: Bring a large pot of water to boil with ginger slices.
Submerge chicken, return to a gentle simmer for 30 minutes.
Plunge into ice water immediately for 15 minutes.
Chop chicken, serve with ginger-scallion oil (hot oil poured over minced ginger and scallions).
tips: Ice bath is critical for the characteristic jelly-like skin.
The chicken should be just barely cooked — slightly pink near bones is traditional.

name: Tanghulu (Candied Fruit Skewers)
desc: Beijing street snack of hawthorn berries coated in crackly sugar glaze
steps: Thread hawthorn berries onto bamboo skewers.
Boil sugar and water until it reaches hard crack stage (150C).
Dip skewered fruit into the hot sugar syrup, rotating to coat.
Place on oiled parchment to cool and harden.
tips: Use a candy thermometer for perfect sugar stage.
Strawberries and grapes are popular modern substitutes for hawthorn.

name: Mango Pudding
desc: Silky Cantonese dim sum dessert of chilled mango with coconut cream
steps: Blend mango flesh until smooth.
Dissolve gelatin in warm water, stir in sugar until dissolved.
Mix gelatin liquid with mango puree and coconut cream.
Pour into molds, refrigerate 4 hours until set.
tips: Use the ripest mangoes for the sweetest, most fragrant result.
A staple dessert at Hong Kong dim sum restaurants.

name: Chinese Tea Eggs (Cha Ye Dan)
desc: Hard-boiled eggs marinated in soy sauce, star anise, and black tea
steps: Hard boil eggs, cool, then gently crack shells all over without peeling.
Simmer soy sauce, tea, spices, sugar, and salt in water.
Add cracked eggs to the liquid, simmer on low for 1 hour.
Let eggs steep in liquid overnight in the fridge for best pattern and flavor.
tips: The marble pattern comes from the cracked shell — crack evenly for best results.
A popular convenience store snack across China and Taiwan.

name: Chinese Zucchini Pancakes (Xi Hu Lu Bing)
desc: Savory shredded zucchini pancakes with egg, a Northern Chinese breakfast
steps: Grate zucchini, salt and squeeze out excess water.
Mix with eggs, flour, scallions, and salt into a batter.
Pan-fry spoonfuls in oil until golden on both sides.
Serve hot with black vinegar dipping sauce.
tips: Squeeze the zucchini very well or pancakes will be soggy.
Common breakfast food in Northern China.

name: Mantou (Chinese Steamed Buns)
desc: Plain steamed bread buns, soft and pillowy, a Northern Chinese staple
steps: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, and water; knead until smooth.
Let dough rise for 1 hour until doubled.
Divide into 10 pieces, shape into smooth rounds.
Steam on high for 15 minutes until puffed and cooked through.
tips: Mantou is the bread of Northern China — served with every meal.
Can be pan-fried the next day for crispy mantou.

name: Home-Style Tofu (Jia Chang Doufu)
desc: Pan-fried tofu braised with pork, peppers, and doubanjiang in a homey sauce
steps: Pan-fry tofu slices until golden on both sides, set aside.
Stir-fry pork slices, add doubanjiang and garlic until fragrant.
Add peppers and tofu back, pour in soy sauce and a splash of water.
Braise 3 minutes until sauce thickens, garnish with scallions.
tips: Jia chang means 'home-style' — this is everyday Sichuan comfort food.
Can omit pork for a vegetarian version.

name: Lo Mai Gai (Sticky Rice in Lotus Leaf)
desc: Glutinous rice with chicken, Chinese sausage, and mushroom steamed in lotus leaf
steps: Soak glutinous rice 4 hours, soak mushrooms and lotus leaves.
Marinate diced chicken with soy and oyster sauce.
Mix rice with sliced sausage, mushrooms, and chicken.
Wrap portions in lotus leaves, steam for 30 minutes.
tips: A classic dim sum dish — the lotus leaf imparts a unique fragrance.
Can be made ahead and resteamed to serve.

name: Sichuan Chili Oil Wonton (Suanla Chaoshou)
desc: Folded pork wontons drenched in mouth-numbing chili oil and black vinegar
steps: Mix pork with a splash of soy sauce, ginger, and sesame oil for filling.
Fold wontons in the chaoshou style (overlapping two corners).
Boil until they float, about 3 minutes.
Serve in a bowl of chili oil, black vinegar, garlic, soy, and peppercorn oil.
tips: Chaoshou means 'crossed hands' — the folding style unique to Sichuan.
Make the sauce while wontons boil.

name: Honey Walnut Shrimp
desc: Crispy battered shrimp with candied walnuts in creamy mayo-honey sauce
steps: Candy walnuts by boiling in sugar water then frying until crispy.
Coat shrimp in cornstarch, deep fry until golden.
Mix mayonnaise, honey, and condensed milk into a creamy sauce.
Toss fried shrimp in the sauce, top with candied walnuts.
tips: A Chinese-American banquet classic.
Make sure shrimp are very dry before coating for maximum crispiness.

name: Braised Tofu Pockets (You Doufu)
desc: Fried tofu puffs braised in soy sauce and star anise until deeply savory
steps: Blanch tofu puffs in hot water to remove excess oil.
Simmer with soy sauce, star anise, sugar, ginger, and stock.
Braise on low heat for 15 minutes until tofu absorbs all the flavors.
Garnish with scallions and serve.
tips: Tofu puffs soak up sauce like sponges — very satisfying.
Great as a side dish or stuffed with minced pork.

name: Chinese Sausage Rice (Lap Cheong Fan)
desc: Fragrant rice cooked with sweet Chinese sausage and soy sauce
steps: Wash rice and place in rice cooker with water and ginger.
Slice sausages diagonally, lay on top of rice before cooking.
Cook rice as normal — the sausage renders fat into the rice.
Blanch bok choy, serve over rice with soy sauce and sesame oil drizzle.
tips: The rendered sausage fat makes the rice incredibly fragrant.
A classic lazy dinner in Cantonese households.

name: Black Sesame Tang Yuan
desc: Glutinous rice balls filled with sweet black sesame paste in ginger syrup
steps: Mix sesame paste with sugar and lard, chill until firm, roll into small balls.
Knead glutinous rice flour with warm water into a smooth dough.
Flatten dough pieces, wrap around sesame filling, roll into smooth balls.
Boil in ginger syrup until they float, serve in the syrup.
tips: Traditionally eaten during Lantern Festival and Winter Solstice.
The filling should ooze out when you bite in.

name: Xinjiang Big Plate Chicken (Da Pan Ji)
desc: Xinjiang braised chicken with potatoes and peppers over hand-pulled noodles
steps: Cut chicken into pieces, sear until golden.
Add dried chilies, cumin, ginger, garlic, soy sauce, and water.
Add chunked potatoes, braise 25 minutes until tender.
Add peppers, cook 5 minutes more, serve over hand-pulled noodles.
tips: The noodles soak up the spicy braising sauce — the best part.
A signature dish of Xinjiang's Muslim Uyghur cuisine.

name: Siu Mai (Pork and Shrimp Dumplings)
desc: Open-topped dim sum dumplings filled with pork, shrimp, and mushroom
steps: Chop shrimp coarsely, mix with pork, diced mushrooms, soy sauce, and sesame oil.
Place filling in the center of wonton wrappers, gather edges around but leave top open.
Garnish each with a piece of shrimp or fish roe on top.
Steam for 10 minutes until cooked through.
tips: Siu mai should be open at the top showing the filling.
A must-order dish at any dim sum restaurant.

name: Spicy Garlic Eggplant (Suan Xiang Qie Zi)
desc: Charred eggplant in garlic-heavy spicy sauce, a Sichuan home-cooking classic
steps: Cut eggplant into batons, fry in oil until charred and soft.
In the same wok, fry garlic and chili flakes until fragrant.
Add soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, and a splash of water.
Return eggplant, toss to coat, garnish with scallions.
tips: Use the long thin Asian eggplant variety for best results.
The heavy garlic is what distinguishes this from other eggplant dishes.

name: Cantonese Lemon Chicken
desc: Crispy battered chicken cutlets glazed with tangy sweet lemon sauce
steps: Butterfly and flatten chicken breasts, coat in egg then flour-cornstarch mix.
Deep fry until golden and crispy, drain and slice.
Make sauce by simmering lemon juice, sugar, and cornstarch slurry.
Pour hot lemon sauce over sliced chicken and serve.
tips: Slice chicken after frying so sauce coats every piece.
Use fresh lemons, not bottled juice.

name: Tomato Beef Noodle Soup
desc: Comforting Chinese noodle soup with beef brisket in tangy tomato broth
steps: Sear beef brisket chunks until browned on all sides.
Add quartered tomatoes, ginger, star anise, soy sauce, and water.
Simmer for 1.5 hours until beef is fall-apart tender.
Cook noodles, ladle soup and beef over, garnish with scallions.
tips: The tomato-based broth is lighter than Taiwanese beef noodle soup.
Use ripe tomatoes for a naturally sweet broth.

name: Buddha's Delight (Luohan Zhai)
desc: Mixed vegetarian stir-fry with tofu, mushrooms, and glass noodles for Lunar New Year
steps: Soak mushrooms and glass noodles until softened.
Stir-fry tofu until golden, set aside.
Stir-fry all vegetables with mushrooms, add soy sauce and stock.
Add glass noodles and tofu, simmer until noodles absorb the sauce.
tips: Traditionally made with 10-18 ingredients for Buddhist vegetarian celebrations.
Each ingredient has symbolic meaning for the new year.

name: Da Bao (Large Steamed Pork Buns)
desc: Big fluffy steamed buns stuffed with seasoned pork and vegetables
steps: Make yeast dough, let rise until doubled.
Salt-wilt cabbage, squeeze dry, mix with pork, soy sauce, ginger, and sesame oil.
Divide dough, flatten pieces, fill with pork mixture, pleat shut.
Steam for 18 minutes until puffed and cooked through.
tips: These are larger than char siu bao — a full meal in a bun.
A Beijing breakfast street food staple.

name: Rou Jia Mo (Chinese Hamburger)
desc: Xi'an-style flatbread stuffed with slow-braised spiced pork
steps: Braise pork with all spices, soy sauce, and wine for 1.5 hours until falling apart.
Make flatbread dough, rest 30 minutes, then form into discs.
Bake or pan-fry flatbreads until golden and slightly crispy.
Shred braised pork, stuff generously into split flatbreads.
tips: Considered one of the oldest 'sandwiches' in the world from Xi'an.
The pork should be so tender it shreds with a spoon.

name: Biang Biang Noodles
desc: Xi'an hand-pulled wide belt noodles with chili oil and garlic
steps: Make dough with flour, water, and salt; rest 1 hour.
Pull and slap dough into wide, flat belt-like noodles.
Boil noodles 2-3 minutes, place in bowls with garlic, chili, and soy.
Heat oil until smoking, pour over the chili and garlic to sizzle and bloom.
tips: The noodles should be wide as a belt — that's the signature.
The sizzling oil hitting the chili is what creates the deep flavor.

name: Moo Shu Pork
desc: Stir-fried shredded pork with wood ear, eggs, and cabbage in thin pancakes
steps: Shred pork, marinate with soy sauce and cornstarch.
Scramble eggs, set aside. Stir-fry pork until cooked.
Add soaked wood ears and shredded cabbage, cook until wilted.
Return eggs, toss together. Serve with warm pancakes, hoisin, and scallions.
tips: Spread hoisin on the pancake first, then add the filling.
Mandarin pancakes can be steamed or dry-fried.

name: Mei Cai Kou Rou (Steamed Pork Belly with Preserved Mustard Greens)
desc: Hakka dish of layered pork belly and mei cai steamed until meltingly tender
steps: Boil whole pork belly until just cooked, rub skin with soy sauce.
Deep fry pork belly skin-side down until blistered, cool and slice.
Layer sliced pork skin-side down in a bowl, top with seasoned mei cai.
Steam for 2 hours, invert onto a plate to reveal the skin.
tips: The long steaming makes pork belly incredibly soft.
A Hakka Chinese celebration dish served at banquets.

name: Jian Dui (Sesame Balls)
desc: Deep-fried glutinous rice balls coated in sesame seeds with red bean filling
steps: Mix glutinous rice flour with sugar and warm water into a dough.
Flatten pieces, fill with red bean paste, roll into smooth balls.
Roll balls in sesame seeds, pressing gently to adhere.
Deep fry on low heat, pressing gently to expand, until golden all over.
tips: Fry on low heat — high heat will burst them.
Press gently with a skimmer while frying to help them puff up.

name: Douhua (Silken Tofu Pudding)
desc: Warm silky soy pudding in sweet ginger syrup, a Cantonese breakfast classic
steps: Heat fresh soy milk to 85C.
Dissolve gypsum powder in water, pour hot soy milk over it quickly.
Cover and let set undisturbed for 15 minutes.
Make ginger syrup by simmering ginger with brown sugar. Spoon tofu pudding into bowls, drizzle with syrup.
tips: Do not stir after pouring — it sets from the heat like a custard.
Popular as breakfast in Guangdong and Hong Kong.

name: Sichuan Pepper Chicken (Jiao Ma Ji)
desc: Cold chicken drenched in intensely numbing Sichuan peppercorn and chili sauce
steps: Poach chicken thighs until just cooked, ice bath, then shred or chop.
Mix peppercorn oil, chili oil, soy sauce, sugar, and minced garlic.
Arrange chicken on a plate, pour the numbing sauce over.
Garnish with scallion slivers and serve cold.
tips: The peppercorn oil is what gives the extreme numbing sensation.
Best served chilled on a hot day.

name: Yangzhou Fried Rice
desc: Classic Jiangsu fried rice with shrimp, char siu, peas, and egg
steps: Beat eggs, scramble in hot wok, break into small pieces.
Stir-fry shrimp and diced char siu until cooked.
Add cold rice, toss on high heat to separate grains.
Add peas, scallions, soy sauce, and return egg. Toss until smoky.
tips: Day-old rice is essential — fresh rice will be mushy.
Yangzhou fried rice is the 'original' Chinese fried rice recipe.

name: Suan Cai Yu (Pickled Vegetable Fish)
desc: Northeast Chinese sour pickled cabbage soup with tender fish fillets
steps: Slice fish, marinate with salt, white pepper, and cornstarch.
Stir-fry pickled cabbage with ginger and garlic until fragrant.
Add stock, bring to a boil, simmer 10 minutes for sour broth.
Gently poach fish slices in the broth until just cooked through.
tips: The sour flavor comes entirely from the fermented cabbage.
A comfort food staple in Dongbei (Northeastern China).

name: Salt Baked Chicken (Yan Ju Ji)
desc: Hakka whole chicken baked in a mound of hot salt for incredibly moist meat
steps: Rub chicken with wine, five-spice, and sand ginger; air-dry 1 hour.
Wrap chicken tightly in parchment paper.
Heat salt in a large pot until very hot, bury the wrapped chicken in salt.
Cover and cook on low heat for 1 hour. Unwrap and chop to serve.
tips: Sand ginger powder (sha jiang) is the signature Hakka spice.
The salt is reusable — store it for next time.

name: Stir-Fried Lotus Root with Vinegar
desc: Crispy lotus root slices tossed in black vinegar and chili, a refreshing side
steps: Peel and slice lotus root thinly, soak in vinegar water to prevent browning.
Stir-fry dried chilies and peppercorns in oil until fragrant.
Add lotus root, stir-fry on high heat for 2 minutes, keeping it crunchy.
Add vinegar and sugar, toss and serve immediately.
tips: Keep the lotus root crunchy — do not overcook.
The vinegar water soak keeps the slices white and crisp.
